factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the structural steel fabrication market but does not mention or map the market to ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221. It also does not explicitly state the relevant ANZSIC classification code anywhere in the analysis.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives an employment figure of approximately 4,100 in 2025 and ~4,011 in 2026, but it does not provide employment figures in the 4,200–4,600 range for multiple years within 2018–2024. It also explicitly notes that annual employment by year (2018–2024) is not available.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives a market value estimate of approximately NZ$1.8 billion (though for 2025/2026) but does not state that 2023–2024 market revenue is approximately NZD 1.4–1.9 billion. It also does not provide a 2023–2024 revenue range that explicitly falls within 1.4–1.9 billion for that period.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states, under “Tonnage of Structural Steel Fabricated,” that sector capacity is estimated at “120,000–150,000 tonnes per annum,” which matches the required domestic fabrication capacity range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists five major fabricators but does not provide any geographic locations (cities/regions) for them. It mentions locations only in project contexts (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch) rather than where fabricators are based or operate. Therefore it fails to give locations for at least three fabricators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions HS codes 7207–7216 generally but does not actually specify any of those HS codes (or a relevant subset/subheadings) as the applicable classifications for structural steel imports. It provides no list of specific HS headings/subheadings within 7207–7216 tied to structural steel products.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that most construction-grade raw steel under HS 7207–7216 is duty-free (0%) and also notes that some tariff lines have a 5% MFN rate for certain steel products, implying exceptions within the range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: In the City Rail Link project section, the response estimates steel demand as “~4,000 tonnes of structural steel (plus ~25,000 tonnes reinforcing steel),” which does not state approximately 30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural. The required reinforcing range (30,000–32,000) is not provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: In the City Rail Link project section, the response states an “operational launch in 2026,” which indicates completion/opening circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives Te Kaha’s estimated structural steel demand as ~16,400 tonnes and does not mention the roof steel being approximately 9,000 tonnes. There is no statement isolating the roof steel quantity for Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium. The specific figure “~9,000 tonnes” is absent.
The response states that Te Kaha – Christchurch Multi-Use Arena has an official opening set for March 27, 2026, which satisfies the requirement to state a 2026 opening.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists three major infrastructure projects (City Rail Link, Te Kaha, Penlink) but does not mention New Dunedin Hospital at all, nor describe it as a major project requiring significant structural steel components.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response presents two conflicting market size figures (IBISWorld NZ$1.8b vs Grand View USD 50.3m) but does not reconcile them by explaining differing scope definitions (e.g., narrow fabrication-only vs broader structural steel products/services). It lacks an explicit discussion of boundary differences causing the discrepancy.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists Steel & Tube and Pacific Steel among the “Top 5 structural steel fabricators” and describes Steel & Tube as having a national distribution network and Pacific Steel as a manufacturer of rebar, but it does not explicitly distinguish these distributors/mills/producers from true construction fabricators in the ranking.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response states that most steel inputs are duty-free and explicitly notes that fabricators are “far more exposed to swings in global prices, shipping rates, and FX risk than to NZ Customs import tariffs,” implying the loss of a tariff-based price umbrella and margin dependence on global mill prices/FX/freight.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not give a concrete, step-by-step method for extracting class-level (industry code) data from Stats NZ/MBIE (e.g., which Stats NZ AES/Infoshare table to download and how to filter by ANZSIC L4 code like C2221). It only states that official time-series data is not available and links generally to Infoshare and manufacturing topics without actionable filtering instructions.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links construction activity contraction in 2024–2025 and falling consents/insolvencies to suppressed order books and notes recovery “post-2026,” tying market/employment conditions to the construction cycle timing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists estimated steel demand for individual projects (e.g., CRL 4,000t structural, Te Kaha 16,400t) but does not aggregate demand across multiple named projects to validate any stated fabrication capacity/throughput. It also provides no calculation comparing summed project tonnage to the cited 120,000–150,000 tpa capacity figure.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes that official annual tonnage time-series data from Stats NZ/MBIE is absent, but it does not propose a specific triangulation method to estimate tonnage using SCNZ capacity, trade/import-export data, or project-level steel disclosures. It only provides a broad capacity/output estimate and flags gaps without outlining a concrete triangulation approach.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response states that import tariffs are mostly 0% and that fabricators are “far more exposed to swings in global prices, shipping rates, and FX risk than to NZ Customs import tariffs,” framing margin pressure around global price/FX exposure rather than NZ border protection.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: Many quantitative figures include units (e.g., NZ$589.1 million, 4,100 people, 120,000–150,000 tonnes per annum, 0%/5% tariffs) and some time markers (FY23, 2024, 2025–2026). However, several numbers lack explicit timeframes or precision (e.g., “~NZ$62.5 million” without year, “~200” without year), and the requested 2018–2024 time-series is not presented with year-by-year units/precision.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is clearly divided into distinct sections for market size and growth, top companies, tariffs and margin impact, major projects, and strategic risks/opportunities, matching each requested dimension.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, analytical tone and generally avoids overtly promotional phrasing. However, it includes speculative/unsupported forward-looking claims (e.g., “projected to shrink,” “expected to rise,” “position NZ suppliers,” “as the construction cycle recovers post-2026”) and some unsubstantiated assertions (e.g., “world-class compliance,” “over NZ$780 million in digital transformation”).

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not provide 2018–2024 employment, revenue, or capacity figures in a table or structured year-by-year format. It only gives point-in-time estimates (e.g., 2025 employment, general capacity ranges) and explicitly notes that annual 2018–2024 time-series data are not available. No year-by-year dataset is presented.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-style tone throughout, using structured headings and technical language. It does not use rhetorical questions or notably colloquial/informal phrasing that would be inappropriate for investment-grade research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is highly verbose, using filler and unnecessary elaboration such as “comprehensive/exhaustive analysis,” long narrative introductions, repeated caveats/notes, and extended contextual commentary that increases length without adding proportional substantive information.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response links to Stats NZ “Manufacturing and Production” and “Infoshare,” but it does not cite Stats NZ Business Demography (or a comparable Stats NZ business demography publication) as the source for employment figures. Employment numbers are attributed to IBISWorld/SCNZ instead, not Stats NZ Business Demography.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite Stats NZ’s Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any equivalent Stats NZ economic survey as the source for revenue/sales data. It links generally to Stats NZ topics and Infoshare, but the revenue figures presented come from IBISWorld/Grand View Research and are not attributed to AES.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites NZ Customs’ “Working Tariff Document” and links to official NZ Customs tariff classification/rates pages when discussing HS 7207–7216 duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response references Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) multiple times for fabrication capacity/throughput data, including citing SCNZ “Statistics” and SCNZ Industry Updates (March 2023, June 2023) as sources for tonnage/capacity estimates.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references the NZ Companies Office as a data source, including multiple “Companies Office” links in the top fabricators section (e.g., Steel & Tube and other firms’ Companies Office entries).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides project-specific steel demand figures and cites sources for City Rail Link (e.g., WSP and NZ Steel links) and Te Kaha (Christchurch City Council update and Wikipedia). These are two major infrastructure projects among the listed options (CRL and Te Kaha) with explicit data sources.
